key_insight: On Au(111), tribromotrioxaazatriangulene does not go directly to a covalent Kagome polymer; it forms Au-adatom–stabilized organometallic intermediates and retains brominated edge terminations up to high temperature.
engineering_relevance: Understanding these intermediate states and edge terminations helps control on-surface synthesis routes for defect- and topology-sensitive 2D pi-conjugated polymers with desired Kagome-lattice properties.
method: Scanning tunneling microscopy to track molecular ordering and growth on Au(111)
High-resolution atomic force microscopy to resolve bond-level structures and edge terminations
Density functional theory to interpret Au-adatom coordination and reaction pathways
evidence_signal: The abstract reports microscopy plus DFT evidence for previously unreported organometallic intermediates, with many polymer edges remaining brominated and/or Au-adatom coordinated up to 250 �b0C.
watch_item: Edge chemistry appears highly substrate- and temperature-dependent, so growth protocols may need tight thermal control to avoid unintended bromine retention or Au-adatom trapping.
paper_title: Mapping the Growth of Two-Dimensional $π$-Conjugated Polymers on Au(111): Organometallic Intermediates and Edge Terminations
